patterns.description:
\usepgflibrary{patterns} % LaTeX and plain TeX and pure pgf \usepgflibrary[patterns] % ConTeXt and pure pgf \usetikzlibrary{patterns} % LaTeX and plain TeX when using TikZ \usetikzlibrary[patterns] % ConTeXt when using TikZ The package defines patterns for filling areas.

by Mark Wibrow

patterns.meta.description:
\usepgflibrary{patterns.meta} % LaTeX and plain TeX and pure pgf \usepgflibrary[patterns.meta] % ConTeXt and pure pgf \usetikzlibrary{patterns.meta} % LaTeX and plain TeX when using TikZ \usetikzlibrary[patterns.meta] % ConTeXt when using TikZ Define your own patterns with a syntax similar to arrows.meta.

Caveat: This library is currently experimental and might change without notice. There are some known shortcomings that will hopefully be fixed in the future.

\\pgfdeclarepattern.description: This command is used to declare a new pattern. In contrast to the normal patterns and in the spirit of arrows.meta this command takes a list of keys and values to define the pattern. The following keys are available:

/pgf/patterns/name.description: The name of the pattern by which it can be used later on.

/pgf/patterns/type.description:
The type of the pattern maps to what was called “form only” and “inherently colored” in the language of the normal patterns. The available choices are:

• uncolored the pattern will obey the surrounding color.

type.description:
The type of the pattern maps to what was called “form only” and “inherently colored” in the language of the normal patterns. The available choices are:

• uncolored the pattern will obey the surrounding color.

/pgf/patterns/x.description: Unit vector of the coordinate system in the \(x\)-direction.

/pgf/patterns/y.description: Unit vector of the coordinate system in the \(y\)-direction.

/pgf/patterns/parameters.description: A list of parameters that are passed to the pattern. This is usually a list of TeX macros. It is very important that these macros are fully expandable because the values they hold are being used for deduplication in the PDF file.

parameters.description: A list of parameters that are passed to the pattern. This is usually a list of TeX macros. It is very important that these macros are fully expandable because the values they hold are being used for deduplication in the PDF file.

/pgf/patterns/defaults.description: This list holds default assignments to the parameters passed to the pattern. The default keys can then be found under the /pgf/pattern keys/ prefix.

defaults.description: This list holds default assignments to the parameters passed to the pattern. The default keys can then be found under the /pgf/pattern keys/ prefix.

/pgf/patterns/bottom left.description: Bottom left corner of the pattern’s bounding box, e.g. \pgfqpoint{-.1pt}{-.1pt}.

bottom left.description: Bottom left corner of the pattern’s bounding box, e.g. \pgfqpoint{-.1pt}{-.1pt}.

/pgf/patterns/top right.description: Top right corner of the pattern’s bounding box, e.g. \pgfqpoint{3.1pt}{3.1pt}.

top right.description: Top right corner of the pattern’s bounding box, e.g. \pgfqpoint{3.1pt}{3.1pt}.

/pgf/patterns/tile size.description: Width and height of a single of the pattern as a pgf point specification, i.e. the \(x\) coordinate is the width and the \(y\) coordinate is the height, e.g. \pgfqpoint{3pt}{3pt}.

tile size.description: Width and height of a single of the pattern as a pgf point specification, i.e. the \(x\) coordinate is the width and the \(y\) coordinate is the height, e.g. \pgfqpoint{3pt}{3pt}.

/pgf/patterns/code.description: The code should be pgf code that can be protocolled. It should not contain any color code or nodes.

/pgf/patterns/set up code.description:
This code can be set if parameters have to be preprocessed before the actual pattern code can be run.

There are a couple of predefined pgf patterns which are similar to their normal counterparts. For all of these the xshift and yshift are applied before the rotation. If you want to rotate before shifting, just rotate in the drawing code.

set up code.description:
This code can be set if parameters have to be preprocessed before the actual pattern code can be run.

There are a couple of predefined pgf patterns which are similar to their normal counterparts. For all of these the xshift and yshift are applied before the rotation. If you want to rotate before shifting, just rotate in the drawing code.

/pgf/pattern keys/angle.description: By default the lines are horizontal. The whole pattern is rotated by this angle. The rotation angle is measured in the mathematically positive sense.

/pgf/pattern keys/xshift.description: Shifts the whole pattern in \(x\)-direction (before applying the rotation).

/pgf/pattern keys/yshift.description: Shifts the whole pattern in \(y\)-direction (before applying the rotation).

/pgf/pattern keys/line width.description:
Thickness of the lines.

The following settings can be used to reproduce the other ... lines patterns.

/pgf/pattern keys/radius.description:
Radius of the dots.

The following settings can be used to reproduce the dots and crosshatch dots patterns.

/pgf/pattern keys/points.description: Number of pointy ends of the stars.

points.description: Number of pointy ends of the stars.

\\tikzdeclarepattern.description: A pattern declared with \pgfdeclarepattern can only execute pgf code. This command extends the functionality to also allow TikZ code. All the same keys of \pgfdeclarepattern are valid, but some of them have been overloaded to give a more natural TikZ syntax.

/tikz/patterns/bottom left.description: Instead of a pgf name point, this key takes a TikZ point, e.g. (-.1,-.1).

/tikz/patterns/top right.description: Instead of a pgf name point, this key takes a TikZ point, e.g. (3.1,3.1).

/tikz/patterns/tile size.description: Instead of a pgf name point, this key takes a TikZ point, e.g. (3,3).

/tikz/patterns/tile transformation.description:
Instead of a pgf transformation, this key takes a list of keys and value and extracts the resulting transformation from them, e.g. rotate=30.

In addition to the overloaded keys, some new keys have been added.

/tikz/patterns/bounding box.description: This is a shorthand to set the bounding box. It will assign the first point to bottom left and the second point to top right.

bounding box.description: This is a shorthand to set the bounding box. It will assign the first point to bottom left and the second point to top right.

/tikz/patterns/infer tile bounding box.description:
Instead of specifying the bounding box by hand, you can ask TikZ to infer the size of the bounding box for you. The ⟨dimension⟩ parameter is padding that is added around the bounding box.

Instead of macros you can also use pgf keys as parameters, if that is what you prefer.

infer tile bounding box.description:
Instead of specifying the bounding box by hand, you can ask TikZ to infer the size of the bounding box for you. The ⟨dimension⟩ parameter is padding that is added around the bounding box.

Instead of macros you can also use pgf keys as parameters, if that is what you prefer.
